name: file-converter
description: This skill handles file format conversions across documents (PDF, DOCX,
capabilities: Generate Python code for document conversions (PDF, DOCX, Markdown, HTML, TXT)
Generate Python code for data file conversions (JSON, CSV, YAML, XML, TOML)
Generate Python code for image conversions (PNG, JPG, WebP, SVG, GIF)
Provide encoding detection and handling for text files
Include quality settings for image compression and format-specific options
Handle edge cases like transparency, nested structures, and color profiles
author: Joseph OBrien
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing code examples and guidance for generating file conversion scripts. All 139 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES because the skill contains no executable code, no file system operations, no network calls, and no credential access. The scanner flagged code examples in documentation as if they were executable, including CLI tool references (libreoffice) and MD5 checksums (for file integrity verification) which are legitimate documentation patterns.
valueStatement: Converting files between formats requires knowledge of specific libraries and their parameters. This skill provides ready-to-use Python code for converting documents, data files, and images between common formats with proper error handling.
limitations: Does not execute conversions directly; generates code for the AI to run
Requires user approval and proper Python environment with libraries installed
Styling fidelity varies between format conversions
Complex conversions like scanned PDFs require additional OCR setup

useCases.title: Convert data between formats
useCases.description: Transform JSON to YAML, CSV to JSON, or handle XML conversions for data pipelines

useCases.title: Export documents to PDF
useCases.description: Convert Markdown or DOCX files to PDF with proper formatting and styling

useCases.title: Optimize images for web
useCases.description: Convert images to WebP or PNG with quality settings and transparency handling

promptTemplates.title: Basic JSON to YAML
promptTemplates.prompt: Convert a JSON file to YAML format using Python with proper Unicode handling

promptTemplates.title: PDF to text
promptTemplates.prompt: Extract all text from a PDF file using pypdf with page-by-page processing

promptTemplates.title: Image optimization
promptTemplates.prompt: Convert PNG images to WebP with quality 80 and maintain transparency

promptTemplates.title: Complex batch conversion
promptTemplates.prompt: Convert all CSV files in a directory to JSON with type inference for numeric and boolean values

bestPractices: Specify encoding explicitly when reading text files to avoid errors
Use proper libraries for each format pair (e.g., mammoth for DOCX to Markdown)
Handle edge cases like transparency for images and nested structures for JSON/CSV conversion
antiPatterns: Using the wrong library for a format pair (e.g., wrong XML converter)
Ignoring encoding issues that cause UnicodeDecodeError on special characters
Attempting to convert complex nested JSON directly to CSV without flattening

faq.question: What formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: Documents: PDF, DOCX, Markdown, HTML, TXT. Data: JSON, CSV, YAML, XML, TOML. Images: PNG, JPG, WebP, SVG, GIF.

faq.question: Are there file size limits?
faq.answer: The skill generates code but actual limits depend on available memory and Python libraries used for conversion.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The generated code runs in the same environment where Claude Code is executed with user approval.

faq.question: Is my data safe during conversion?
faq.answer: The skill only generates code. All file operations run locally on your machine with your approval.

faq.question: Conversion fails with encoding error. What now?
faq.answer: Use the chardet library to detect encoding, then specify it explicitly when opening files.

faq.question: How is this different from online converters?
faq.answer: This skill runs locally using Python libraries. Your files never leave your machine.
